stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The axolotl is a salamander native to Mexico.
- Unlike most amphibians, it never undergoes metamorphosis.
- It keeps its gills and fins its entire life.
- This condition is called neoteny.

The student wants to introduce the axolotl to an audience unfamiliar with it. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: The axolotl is a Mexican salamander that, unusually, keeps its gills and fins its entire life instead of transforming like other amphibians.
The axolotl exhibits neoteny throughout its lifespan.
Neoteny is a condition found in the axolotl.
The axolotl never undergoes metamorphosis.

explanation: A introduces what the axolotl is, where it lives, and the unusual trait in plain language — exactly what an unfamiliar audience needs. The other options assume prior knowledge (B, C mention neoteny without defining it) or omit context (D).

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The Hagia Sophia was built in Constantinople in 537 CE.
- It served as a cathedral for nearly 900 years.
- After 1453, it became a mosque.
- Today it is a working mosque again after a period as a museum.

The student wants to emphasize how the Hagia Sophia's function has changed over time. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Since its construction in 537 CE, the Hagia Sophia has served as a cathedral, a mosque, a museum, and once again a mosque.
The Hagia Sophia, built in 537 CE, is located in Constantinople.
The Hagia Sophia became a mosque in 1453.
Visitors today can see the Hagia Sophia in Istanbul.

explanation: A lists the sequence of functions the building has served, directly addressing how its purpose has shifted. The other options mention only one role or none.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Bamboo is technically a grass, not a tree.
- Some species can grow over three feet in a single day.
- It is one of the fastest-growing plants on Earth.
- It is used as a building material across Asia.

The student wants to emphasize bamboo's rapid growth. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Bamboo is used as a building material across Asia.
Bamboo is classified as a grass rather than a tree.
Bamboo, one of the fastest-growing plants on Earth, can gain more than three feet of height in a single day.
Bamboo has many uses in construction.

explanation: C pairs the claim (fastest-growing) with the concrete measurement (three feet per day), the two notes that speak directly to growth speed.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The Sahara is the largest hot desert in the world.
- It covers about 3.6 million square miles.
- That is roughly the size of the United States.
- It spans parts of eleven African countries.

The student wants to help readers grasp the Sahara's size. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: The Sahara spans parts of eleven African countries.
The Sahara is a hot desert in Africa.
The Sahara is very large.
Covering about 3.6 million square miles — an area roughly the size of the United States — the Sahara is the largest hot desert in the world.

explanation: D combines the raw figure (3.6 million square miles) with a familiar comparison (U.S.-sized), giving readers two ways to picture the scale.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The fennec fox lives in the Sahara Desert.
- It has very large ears compared to its body size.
- The ears help release body heat to keep it cool.
- It is the smallest of all fox species.

The student wants to explain how the fennec fox is adapted to its environment. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: The fennec fox, native to the Sahara, uses its oversized ears to release body heat and stay cool in the desert climate.
The fennec fox lives in the Sahara Desert.
The fennec fox is the smallest fox species.
The fennec fox has large ears.

explanation: A links the environment (Sahara) to the trait (large ears) to the function (heat release), showing adaptation as a cause-and-effect relationship.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The kakapo is a flightless parrot from New Zealand.
- It is nocturnal and can live over 90 years.
- Fewer than 250 kakapos remain alive.
- Conservationists track each bird individually.

The student wants to emphasize how rare the kakapo is. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: The kakapo is a nocturnal parrot from New Zealand.
So few kakapos remain — fewer than 250 in total — that conservationists track every individual bird.
The kakapo can live over 90 years.
The kakapo is a flightless bird.

explanation: B uses the population figure (fewer than 250) and the individual-tracking detail — the two notes that speak directly to rarity.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Pompeii was a Roman city in southern Italy.
- In 79 CE, Mount Vesuvius erupted and buried it in ash.
- The ash preserved buildings, frescoes, and everyday objects.
- Archaeologists began uncovering the site in the 1700s.

The student wants to introduce Pompeii and why it matters to archaeologists. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Pompeii was a Roman city in Italy.
Mount Vesuvius erupted in 79 CE.
Excavation of Pompeii began in the 1700s.
Pompeii, a Roman city buried by Vesuvius in 79 CE, remains valuable to archaeologists because the volcanic ash preserved its buildings, frescoes, and everyday objects.

explanation: D identifies Pompeii, the event, and the reason it matters — a preservation effect specific to the ash — all in one sentence.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The tardigrade is a microscopic animal often called a "water bear."
- It can survive temperatures from near absolute zero to above boiling.
- It has been revived after exposure to the vacuum of space.
- It enters a dormant state called cryptobiosis to withstand extremes.

The student wants to make a case that tardigrades are among the most resilient animals known. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Tardigrades, nicknamed water bears, are microscopic.
Tardigrades can be found in moss and lichen around the world.

By entering cryptobiosis, tardigrades have survived temperatures from near absolute zero to above boiling and even exposure to the vacuum of space — a range of conditions that would kill nearly any other animal.
The tardigrade enters a dormant state called cryptobiosis.

explanation: C marshals multiple extreme-condition notes and the mechanism (cryptobiosis) into one claim about resilience. The other options give isolated facts without supporting the argument.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Octopuses have three hearts and blue, copper-based blood.
- Two hearts pump blood to the gills; the third pumps it to the rest of the body.
- The body heart stops beating when the octopus swims.
- Swimming is tiring for octopuses, so they usually prefer to crawl.

The student wants to explain why octopuses tend to crawl rather than swim. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Because an octopus's main body heart stops beating when it swims, swimming quickly exhausts the animal, which is why octopuses generally prefer to crawl.
Octopuses have blue, copper-based blood.
Octopuses crawl because they have three hearts.
Two of the octopus's hearts pump blood to its gills.

explanation: A chains the mechanism (heart stops) → effect (exhaustion) → behavior (crawling), which is the causal story the prompt asks for.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Amelia Earhart was the first woman to fly solo across the Atlantic (1932).
- She set several aviation records for speed and distance.
- In 1937, she attempted to fly around the world at the equator.
- Her plane disappeared over the Pacific; it has never been found.

The student wants to contrast Earhart's achievements with her final flight. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Though Amelia Earhart set multiple aviation records and flew solo across the Atlantic in 1932, her 1937 attempt to circle the globe ended in mystery when her plane vanished over the Pacific and was never recovered.
Amelia Earhart disappeared over the Pacific in 1937.
Earhart was a famous pilot who set many records.
Earhart flew solo across the Atlantic in 1932.

explanation: A pairs the successes (records, Atlantic solo) with the tragic final flight, creating the contrast the student wants.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Great white sharks are often portrayed as indiscriminate human-eaters.
- Fatal attacks on humans are extremely rare — fewer than 10 per year worldwide.
- Most bites are thought to be investigatory, not predatory.
- Humans kill roughly 100 million sharks per year through fishing.

The student wants to challenge the popular image of great whites as a major threat to humans. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Great white sharks are apex predators in many marine ecosystems.
Great white sharks are often feared by swimmers.
Contrary to their reputation, great whites fatally attack fewer than ten people worldwide each year and most bites appear investigatory — while humans kill roughly 100 million sharks annually.
Fatal shark attacks are rare.

explanation: C pushes back on the popular image using three relevant notes (low fatalities, investigatory bites, human toll), a direct counter-argument.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Venus and Earth are similar in size, mass, and composition.
- Both planets formed around the same time, about 4.5 billion years ago.
- Venus's surface is hot enough to melt lead (about 465 °C).
- A runaway greenhouse effect trapped heat in Venus's thick CO₂ atmosphere.

The student wants to emphasize how different Venus became from Earth despite their similarities. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Venus's atmosphere is made mostly of carbon dioxide.
Venus is sometimes called Earth's sister planet.
Though Venus and Earth formed around the same time and share similar size and composition, a runaway greenhouse effect turned Venus's CO₂ atmosphere into a furnace hot enough to melt lead.
Venus has a surface temperature of about 465 °C.

explanation: C sets up the similarity (formation, size, composition) and then the dramatic divergence (runaway greenhouse, lead-melting heat), which is the contrast the prompt asks for.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Jazz pianist Mary Lou Williams composed and arranged for Duke Ellington and Benny Goodman.
- She mentored younger musicians, including Thelonious Monk and Dizzy Gillespie.
- She performed professionally for over 50 years.
- Critics have called her one of jazz's most influential figures.

The student wants to highlight Williams's influence on other musicians. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Mary Lou Williams was active in jazz for more than 50 years.
Williams was a skilled pianist and composer.
Williams shaped jazz from behind the keys — arranging for Ellington and Goodman while mentoring future giants such as Thelonious Monk and Dizzy Gillespie.
Critics regarded Williams as an influential musician.

explanation: C names the specific musicians she arranged for and mentored, showing influence through concrete relationships rather than a general claim.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The Voyager 1 probe launched in 1977.
- In 2012, it became the first human-made object to enter interstellar space.
- It is now over 15 billion miles from Earth.
- Its radio signals take more than 22 hours to reach Earth.

The student wants to convey the immense distance Voyager 1 has traveled. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Voyager 1 launched in 1977 and is still operating today.
Voyager 1 entered interstellar space in 2012.
Voyager 1 is now more than 15 billion miles from Earth — so far that its radio signals need over 22 hours to reach us.
Voyager 1 is a spacecraft operated by NASA.

explanation: C combines the distance figure (15 billion miles) with the signal-travel-time (22 hours), turning an abstract number into a tangible sense of scale.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The giant sequoia is one of the largest tree species on Earth.
- A mature sequoia's bark can be over two feet thick.
- The thick bark contains tannins that resist fire and insects.
- Sequoia cones only open to release seeds after exposure to fire.

The student wants to explain how fire plays a role in the giant sequoia's life cycle. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Giant sequoias are among the largest trees in the world.
Sequoia bark can be more than two feet thick.
Giant sequoias have evolved with fire — their thick, tannin-rich bark shields them from flames, while their cones only release seeds after the heat of a fire.
Fire is common in sequoia forests.

explanation: C captures both sides of the fire relationship — protection (bark) and reproduction (heat-opened cones) — which is what a life-cycle explanation needs.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Coral reefs cover less than 1% of the ocean floor.
- They support roughly 25% of all marine species.
- Warming waters cause coral bleaching, which can kill corals.
- Reefs provide coastal protection, food, and tourism income for many communities.

The student wants to argue that protecting reefs matters far beyond their physical footprint. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Coral bleaching is caused by warming waters.
Reefs are an important part of ocean ecosystems.

Coral reefs cover less than 1% of the ocean floor, yet they shelter about a quarter of marine species and provide food, coastal protection, and income to communities — which is why bleaching threatens far more than coral itself.
Coral reefs support 25% of marine species.

explanation: C uses the "less than 1% / yet 25%" contrast plus human benefits to argue that reef loss reaches far beyond the reef itself.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Photographer Gordon Parks began his career shooting fashion in the 1940s.
- He later documented poverty and segregation for Life magazine.
- He wrote novels, memoirs, and poetry.
- He directed the 1971 film Shaft.

The student wants to emphasize the range of creative forms Parks worked in. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Gordon Parks is best remembered as a photographer for Life magazine.
Parks's photographs often addressed poverty and segregation.
Parks directed a film called Shaft in 1971.
Gordon Parks worked across creative media — from fashion and documentary photography to novels, poetry, and the direction of the 1971 film Shaft.

explanation: D names four distinct creative forms Parks practiced (photography, novels, poetry, film direction), which is exactly what "range of creative forms" requires. The other options stay in one lane.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Physicist Chien-Shiung Wu was born in China in 1912 and moved to the U.S. in 1936.
- She developed the Wu experiment in 1956, which disproved the long-held "conservation of parity" law.
- The theorists who proposed the idea she tested won the 1957 Nobel Prize in Physics.
- Wu herself was not included in the prize.

The student wants to highlight a tension between Wu's contribution to physics and the recognition she received. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Chien-Shiung Wu was a Chinese-born physicist who emigrated to the United States.

Although Chien-Shiung Wu's 1956 experiment provided the decisive evidence that overturned the conservation of parity, the 1957 Nobel Prize went only to the theorists whose idea she tested — not to Wu herself.
Wu's experiment disproved the conservation of parity.
The Nobel Prize in Physics is awarded each year in Stockholm.

explanation: B foregrounds Wu's crucial experimental role and the fact that the Nobel excluded her, producing the contrast the student asked for.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- Lichens are made of a fungus and an alga (or cyanobacterium) living together.
- The fungus provides structure and water retention; the alga produces sugars via photosynthesis.
- Lichens grow on bare rock, arctic soil, and other surfaces where little else survives.
- They are among the first organisms to colonize freshly exposed surfaces after glaciers retreat.

The student wants to argue that lichens' success in harsh environments comes directly from their partnership. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Lichens are partnerships of a fungus and an alga.
Lichens can be found growing on rocks and arctic soil.
Lichens colonize freshly exposed surfaces after glaciers retreat.

Lichens' ability to thrive on bare rock and post-glacial soils depends on their symbiosis: the fungus anchors and holds water while the alga supplies sugars through photosynthesis, letting the pair survive where neither partner could alone.

explanation: D spells out each partner's contribution and ties it directly to survival in harsh environments — the exact causal argument the prompt requires.

stem:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

- The Aral Sea was once the fourth-largest lake in the world.
- In the 1960s, Soviet planners diverted its feeder rivers to irrigate cotton fields.
- By 2014, the eastern basin had dried up completely.
- Fishing villages that once lined its shores are now stranded miles from any water.

The student wants to present the Aral Sea's shrinkage as a direct consequence of human decisions. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: The Aral Sea's eastern basin dried up completely in 2014.

The Aral Sea, once the world's fourth-largest lake, has shrunk so severely that its eastern basin dried out by 2014 — the result of Soviet-era decisions to divert its feeder rivers for cotton irrigation, leaving former fishing villages stranded miles inland.
The Aral Sea was once the fourth-largest lake in the world.
Fishing villages near the Aral Sea are now miles from the water.

explanation: B traces the cause (river diversion for cotton) to the effect (drying basin, stranded villages), framing the loss as a human-driven outcome rather than a passive event.
